sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 82, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 81 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't complete the level. Let me analyze the container boundary more carefully to understand the chamber geometry.
Looking at the boundary, I can trace the container shape:
- Points 0-1: [9,19] to [9,48] - top edge
- Points 1-5: right side going down
- Points 5-9: bottom-right area
- Points 9-13: [58,39] to [44,34] - bottom-left of right chamber
- Points 13-17: [44,34] to [39,44] - upper part of right chamber
- Points 17-25: [39,48] to [19,39] - middle area
- Points 25-31: [19,39] to [13,29] - upper-left area
- Points 31-42: [14,28] to [58,9] - left side
- Points 42-54: [58,9] to [13,19] - bottom-left area
Let me focus on the key insight: the container has TWO chambers separated by a wall. The left chamber is yellow, the right chamber is orange. Each chamber has a sky blue square that can move within it.
The key question is: what is the goal? Maybe I need to position the squares at specific locations.
Let me think about the boundary more carefully. The boundary traces the outer edge of the black container. The interior is the playable area (yellow/orange).
For the LEFT chamber:
- Top: around row 14 (boundary points 52-54: [14,14] to [13,19])
- Bottom: around row 58 (boundary points 41-42: [58,28] to [58,9])
- Left: around col 9 (boundary points 42-43: [58,9] to [39,9])
- Right: around col 28 (boundary points 31-32: [14,28] to [18,28])
Wait, but the left square is at row 19, and the boundary shows [19,9] to [19,13] (points 49-50), which suggests there's a wall or boundary at row 19 in the left chamber. This might be a horizontal divider.
Let me re-examine the boundary for the left chamber:
- Points 49-54: [19,9] to [13,19] - this traces the upper-left corner
- Points 34-38: [38,18] to [44,24] - this traces the lower-left area
- Points 41-48: [58,28] to [28,9] - this traces the lower-left corner
Hmm, the left chamber seems to have a complex shape with a divider at around row 19. The left square is at row 19, which might be the boundary between the upper and lower sections.
Wait, I think I see it now. The boundary points 34-35: [38,18] to [38,18]... no, [18,19] to [38,18]. Let me re-read:
- Point 33: [18,19]
- Point 34: [38,18]
- Point 35: [39,19]
So from [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical wall at col 18-19, going from row 18 to row 38. This is the right wall of the left chamber's lower section.
And points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13], this is a horizontal segment at row 19, cols 9-13. This might be the top of the left chamber's lower section.
So the left chamber has:
- Upper section: rows 13-19, cols 19-28 (approximately)
- Lower section: rows 19-58, cols 9-18 (approximately)
The left square is at row 19, col 19, which is at the boundary between the upper and lower sections. It can move up to row 13 (or 19?) in the upper section, and... wait, the square is at col 19, which is in the upper section (cols 19-28). But the lower section is at cols 9-18. So the square can't move from the upper section to the lower section because they're at different columns.
Hmm, but the square moved from col 19 to col 9 using LEFT. So it can move horizontally across the boundary. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the boundary is more complex. Let me trace the left chamber more carefully:
- Points 49-54: [19,9] -> [19,13] -> [18,14] -> [14,14] -> [14,18] -> [13,19]
  This traces: row 19, cols 9-13; then up to row 18, col 14; then up to row 14, col 14; then right to col 18; then up to row 13, col 19.
  This is the upper-left corner of the container.
- Points 54-0: [13,19] -> [9,19]
  This is the top-left corner, going up to row 9.
- Points 0-1: [9,19] -> [9,48]
  This is the top edge.
So the top of the left chamber is at row 9 (not row 13 or 14). The square at row 19 is not at the top of the chamber.
Wait, but the square couldn't move UP from row 19. Let me check: the boundary has [19,9] to [19,13], which is at row 19. But the top of the chamber is at row 9. So there must be a wall or divider at row 19 that blocks upward movement.
Looking at points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13], this is a horizontal segment at row 19. And points 33-34: [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical segment at col 18-19. So there's a wall at row 19, cols 9-13, and a wall at col 18-19, rows 18-38.
I think the left chamber has a "L" shape or something similar. The upper part is at cols 19-28, rows 9-19. The lower part is at cols 9-18, rows 19-58. The square can move within the chamber, but the shape constrains its movement.
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The boundary traces the OUTER edge of the black container. The interior (yellow/orange) is INSIDE the boundary. So the boundary points define the edge between the black container and the interior.
If the boundary has [19,9] to [19,13], this means at row 19, cols 9-13, there's a transition from black to interior. So the interior is below row 19 (or above, depending on the direction).
Let me think about this differently. The boundary is a clockwise traversal. Starting from [9,19] (top-left), going right to [9,48] (top-right), then down, then left, then up, etc.
Points 49-54: [19,9] -> [19,13] -> [18,14] -> [14,14] -> [14,18] -> [13,19]
This is going from bottom-left to top-left of the left chamber. The direction is: right (19,9 to 19,13), then up-right (19,13 to 18,14), then up (18,14 to 14,14), then right (14,14 to 14,18), then up-right (14,18 to 13,19).
So the interior is to the RIGHT of this boundary (since we're tracing clockwise, the interior is on the right). This means the interior of the left chamber is at cols > 9 (for row 19), cols > 13 (for row 19), etc.
Hmm, this is getting too complicated. Let me just try practical moves and see what happens.
Key insight from the boundary: the left chamber has a narrow upper section (around cols 19-28) and a wider lower section (around cols 9-28). The square at row 19, col 19 is in the upper section.
If I move the square LEFT to col 14 or 9, it might enter the lower section, where it can move down. Let me try this!
Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- LEFT: left moves to col 14. If the chamber allows, the square might be able to move down at col 14.
- Then DOWN: if left is now in the lower section, it might be able to move down.
But wait, DOWN moves the LOWER square (higher row number). The right square is at row 54, which is lower than the left square at row 19. So DOWN would move the right square down, not the left square.
Unless... moving the left square to col 14 changes its vertical position? No, LEFT only changes the column, not the row.
Hmm, but what if the chamber at col 14 has a different vertical range? If the left square at col 14 can be at rows 19-58, then it's still at row 19, which is higher than the right square at row 54.
I think the key insight is: to move the left square down, I need it to be the LOWER square. But the right square is always at row >= 39, and the left square is at row 19. So the left square is always higher.
Unless I can move the right square ABOVE the left square. The right square's minimum row is 39 (at col 44) or 44 (at col 34). The left square is at row 19. So the right square can never be above the left square.
Wait, but what if I move the left square DOWN first? But I can't move it down because it's the higher square.
This is a true deadlock. The only way out is if there's a mechanic I'm missing.
Let me reconsider the movement semantics. Maybe UP and DOWN don't work the way I think. Let me re-examine the evidence:
From the history:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39 (both moved up)
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34 (both moved up)
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29 (both moved up)
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 44 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 49 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 49: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 54 (only right moved)
So the pattern is:
- When both at same row: both move
- When at different rows: only the one that would increase separation moves
UP increases vertical separation by moving the higher square up.
DOWN increases vertical separation by moving the lower square down.
This is consistent with my understanding. The problem is that from the current position (left at 19, right at 54), UP would move left up (blocked) and DOWN would move right down (blocked).
Wait, but what about LEFT and RIGHT? They change the horizontal positions. Maybe at different horizontal positions, the vertical bounds are different.
Let me check: at col 9, can the left square go below row 19? The boundary shows [19,9] to [19,13], which is at row 19. But the chamber continues below row 19 at cols 9-18 (based on the boundary points 41-48).
So if the left square is at col 9, it might be able to move down to rows 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 58. But the issue is that DOWN only moves the lower square, and the right square is at row 54.
Unless... I move the right square to a column where its vertical range is different. For example, if the right square at col 49 has a vertical range of 29-49, then it could be at row 29, which is higher than the left square at row 19. Then DOWN would move the left square down!
Let me check the boundary for the right chamber at col 49:
- Points 3-4: [14,49] to [14,53] - top-right corner
- Points 4-5: [14,53] to [53,53] - right edge
- Points 18-19: [38,49] to [29,49] - left edge of upper-right section
So at col 49, the chamber goes from row 14 to row 53 (approximately). The right square at col 49 could be at rows 19, 24, 29, ..., 49, 54.
Wait, but the right square's minimum row is 39 (at col 44). At col 49, the minimum row might be 29 (based on the boundary point [29,49]).
If the right square can be at row 29 at col 49, and the left square is at row 19, then the right square is lower (29 > 19), and DOWN would move the right square down. But I want to move the left square down, so I need the left square to be lower.
Hmm, this still doesn't help. The left square is at row 19, which is the minimum for the left chamber. It can't go lower than row 19... or can it?
Wait, let me re-examine. The left square started at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So row 19 is the TOP of the left chamber (for the column where the square is). But at different columns, the top might be different.
Looking at the boundary:
- Points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13] - at row 19, cols 9-13
- Points 50-51: [19,13] to [18,14] - step up
- Points 51-52: [18,14] to [14,14] - up to row 14
- Points 52-53: [14,14] to [14,18] - right to col 18
- Points 53-54: [14,18] to [13,19] - step up to row 13
So at col 9-13, the top is at row 19. At col 14-18, the top is at row 14. At col 19, the top is at row 13 (or 9?).
Wait, points 54-0: [13,19] to [9,19], this goes up to row 9 at col 19. So at col 19, the top is at row 9.
But the left square is at row 19, col 19, and it couldn't move UP. This suggests that the square is blocked from moving up, even though the chamber extends to row 9.
Oh! Maybe the square is blocked by the chamber shape. At col 19, the chamber might have a narrow passage that the square can't fit through. Or maybe there's a wall at row 19 that blocks upward movement.
Actually, looking at the boundary points 33-34: [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical wall at col 18-19, going from row 18 to row 38. So at col 19, there's a wall from row 18 to row 38. This would block the square from moving up from row 19.
Wait, but the square is at rows 19-23 (5x5 square). If there's a wall at row 18, the square can't move up because its top would be at row 14, which would overlap with the wall at row 18.
Hmm, but the square moved from row 44 to row 19 using UP. So it could move up to row 19. But it can't move up further because of the wall at row 18.
OK so the left square's vertical range at col 19 is 19-44 (or higher). And at col 9, the vertical range might be different.
Let me check the boundary for col 9:
- Points 42-43: [58,9] to [39,9] - left edge, rows 39-58
- Points 43-44: [39,9] to [39,13] - step right
- Points 48-49: [28,9] to [19,9] - left edge, rows 19-28
So at col 9, the chamber goes from row 19 to row 58. The square at col 9 can be at rows 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, 49, 54.
But the square is currently at row 19, col 19. If I move it LEFT to col 14, then LEFT to col 9, it would still be at row 19. And DOWN would move the right square (at row 54) down, not the left square.
I'm stuck. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is not to move the squares to specific positions, but to achieve some other condition. Let me think about what SPACE does.
Earlier, I tried SPACE and it changed the board (board_changed: True) but didn't complete the level. Let me check what SPACE does.
Actually, looking at the current frame, SPACE might toggle something or change the HUD bars. Let me check the current state after SPACE.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the squares so they touch or overlap. Or maybe the goal is to move the squares to positions where they align with the chamber walls in a specific way.
Let me try moving the squares to their extreme positions and see if SPACE completes the level.
Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- Left is at the top of its chamber (row 19) and rightmost column (col 19).
- Right is at the bottom of its chamber (row 54) and leftmost column (col 34).
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to the opposite corners? Or to specific positions that match the HUD pattern?
Let me try: move left square to bottom-left (row 54, col 9) and right square to top-right (row 39, col 49). But I can't move the left square down...
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me use BFS to search for a sequence of moves that gets both squares to the same row, which would allow them to move together.
State: (left_row, left_col, right_row, right_col)
Actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT
But the state space is large, and I need to know the vertical bounds at each column. Let me estimate:
- Left square: rows 19-54 (at col 9), 19-44 (at col 19)
- Right square: rows 39-54 (at col 44), 29-53 (at col 49), 44-53 (at col 34)
Actually, I realize I don't know the exact vertical bounds at each column. Let me just try to explore the state space by moving the squares to different positions and observing the results.
Plan: Move left square to col 9 (LEFT, LEFT), then try UP to see if the right square can move up (since left is at col 9, maybe its vertical range is different).
Wait, UP moves the higher square up. If left is at row 19 and right is at row 54, left is higher, and UP would move left up (blocked at row 19). So UP won't help.
Let me try: LEFT, LEFT to move left to col 9, then RIGHT to move right to col 39. Then check if the vertical bounds change.
Actually, I just realized something. The right square is at col 34, and the boundary shows the chamber at col 34 goes from row 44 to row 53. But the right square is at row 54, which is below the chamber. This suggests that the right square moved down past the chamber boundary.
Wait, maybe the boundary I'm seeing is for the current configuration, and it changes as the squares move. Or maybe the boundary includes the squares themselves.
Let me re-examine. The container boundary has 55 points. The container has 1325 pixels. The squares have 25 pixels each. So the container is much larger than the squares.
I think the boundary is the outer edge of the black container, and the squares move within the interior (yellow/orange) area. The squares can't move outside the container.
OK, let me try a practical approach. I'll move the left square to col 9 and see if its vertical range changes. Then I'll try to move it down.
But wait, I can't move the left square down because it's the higher square. The only way to move it down is if both squares are at the same row, then DOWN moves both down.
To get both to the same row, I need to either:
1. Move the left square down to match the right square's row.
2. Move the right square up to match the left square's row.
Option 1 requires the left square to be the lower square, which it's not.
Option 2 requires the right square to be the higher square, which it's not.
The only way to break the deadlock is if horizontal movement changes the vertical bounds in a way that allows one square to pass the other.
Let me think about this more carefully. If the left square at col 9 can be at rows 19-58, and the right square at col 49 can be at rows 29-53, then:
- Left at row 19, right at row 54: left is higher, right is lower. Deadlock.
- If I could move the right square to row 29 (at col 49), then right would be at row 29, which is lower than left at row 19. Still deadlock.
- If I could move the left square to row 54 (at col 9), then left would be at row 54, which is lower than right at row 29. Then DOWN would move left down, and UP would move right up.
But I can't move the left square to row 54 because it's stuck at row 19.
Hmm, what if I move the right square to a column where its vertical range includes rows below 19? For example, if the right square at col 49 can be at rows 14-53, then it could be at row 14, which is higher than left at row 19. Then UP would move the right square up (blocked at 14), and DOWN would move the left square down!
Let me check: can the right square reach row 14? The boundary shows [14,49] to [14,53], which is at row 14. So the chamber at col 49-53 has a top at row 14. If the right square can be at row 14, then it would be higher than the left square at row 19.
But the right square's current minimum row is 39 (at col 44). Can it go higher at col 49?
Let me try: move right square to col 49 (LEFT, LEFT from col 34... wait, LEFT moves right square right, so from col 34, LEFT would move it to col 39, then col 44, then col 49).
But LEFT also moves the left square left. Current: left at col 19, right at col 34.
- LEFT: left to col 14, right to col 39
- LEFT: left to col 9, right to col 44
- LEFT: left blocked at col 9, right to col 49
So after 3x LEFT, left is at col 9 and right is at col 49. Then I can try UP to see if the right square can move up.
But UP moves the higher square (left at row 19) up, which is blocked. So the right square wouldn't move.
Unless... at col 9, the left square's vertical range is different. If the left square at col 9 can be at rows 24-58 (with a higher minimum), then the right square at row 54 would be higher than the left square at row 24. Then UP would move the right square up!
But I don't know the vertical range at col 9. Let me just try it.
Plan: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT to move left to col 9 and right to col 49. Then try UP to see if the right square can move up.
Wait, but the left square is at col 19, and LEFT moves it to col 14, then col 9. After 2x LEFT, left is at col 9 and right is at col 44. After 3x LEFT, left is blocked at col 9 and right moves to col 49.
Let me try this sequence and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34)
# Plan: Move left to col 9 and right to col 49, then check if vertical bounds change

# LEFT moves: left col-5, right col+5
# From (19, 19) and (54, 34):
# LEFT 1: left (19, 14), right (54, 39)
# LEFT 2: left (19, 9), right (54, 44)
# LEFT 3: left blocked at 9, right (54, 49)

action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")

# Now try UP - if right square can move up at col 49, that would be progress
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 2x UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 2x UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 3x UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 3x UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Moves HIGHER square (lower row) up by 5; if tied, both move up", "DOWN": "Moves LOWER square (higher row) down by 5; if tied, both move down", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: row 19-54, col 9-19. Right: row 29-54, col 34-49. DEADLOCK at current position.", "goal_model": "Unknown - trying to find target config.", "recent_findings": ["SPACE changes board but doesn't complete level", "Need to get both squares to same row to move together"], "open_questions": ["Can right square go above row 39 at col 49?"], "current_plan": "Move right to col 49, test if it can move above row 39.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE at current config", "DOWN from deadlock", "UP from deadlock"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 81 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't complete the level. Let me analyze the container boundary more carefully to understand the chamber geometry.
Looking at the boundary, I can trace the container shape:
- Points 0-1: [9,19] to [9,48] - top edge
- Points 1-5: right side going down
- Points 5-9: bottom-right area
- Points 9-13: [58,39] to [44,34] - bottom-left of right chamber
- Points 13-17: [44,34] to [39,44] - upper part of right chamber
- Points 17-25: [39,48] to [19,39] - middle area
- Points 25-31: [19,39] to [13,29] - upper-left area
- Points 31-42: [14,28] to [58,9] - left side
- Points 42-54: [58,9] to [13,19] - bottom-left area
Let me focus on the key insight: the container has TWO chambers separated by a wall. The left chamber is yellow, the right chamber is orange. Each chamber has a sky blue square that can move within it.
The key question is: what is the goal? Maybe I need to position the squares at specific locations.
Let me think about the boundary more carefully. The boundary traces the outer edge of the black container. The interior is the playable area (yellow/orange).
For the LEFT chamber:
- Top: around row 14 (boundary points 52-54: [14,14] to [13,19])
- Bottom: around row 58 (boundary points 41-42: [58,28] to [58,9])
- Left: around col 9 (boundary points 42-43: [58,9] to [39,9])
- Right: around col 28 (boundary points 31-32: [14,28] to [18,28])
Wait, but the left square is at row 19, and the boundary shows [19,9] to [19,13] (points 49-50), which suggests there's a wall or boundary at row 19 in the left chamber. This might be a horizontal divider.
Let me re-examine the boundary for the left chamber:
- Points 49-54: [19,9] to [13,19] - this traces the upper-left corner
- Points 34-38: [38,18] to [44,24] - this traces the lower-left area
- Points 41-48: [58,28] to [28,9] - this traces the lower-left corner
Hmm, the left chamber seems to have a complex shape with a divider at around row 19. The left square is at row 19, which might be the boundary between the upper and lower sections.
Wait, I think I see it now. The boundary points 34-35: [38,18] to [38,18]... no, [18,19] to [38,18]. Let me re-read:
- Point 33: [18,19]
- Point 34: [38,18]
- Point 35: [39,19]
So from [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical wall at col 18-19, going from row 18 to row 38. This is the right wall of the left chamber's lower section.
And points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13], this is a horizontal segment at row 19, cols 9-13. This might be the top of the left chamber's lower section.
So the left chamber has:
- Upper section: rows 13-19, cols 19-28 (approximately)
- Lower section: rows 19-58, cols 9-18 (approximately)
The left square is at row 19, col 19, which is at the boundary between the upper and lower sections. It can move up to row 13 (or 19?) in the upper section, and... wait, the square is at col 19, which is in the upper section (cols 19-28). But the lower section is at cols 9-18. So the square can't move from the upper section to the lower section because they're at different columns.
Hmm, but the square moved from col 19 to col 9 using LEFT. So it can move horizontally across the boundary. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the boundary is more complex. Let me trace the left chamber more carefully:
- Points 49-54: [19,9] -> [19,13] -> [18,14] -> [14,14] -> [14,18] -> [13,19]
  This traces: row 19, cols 9-13; then up to row 18, col 14; then up to row 14, col 14; then right to col 18; then up to row 13, col 19.
  This is the upper-left corner of the container.
- Points 54-0: [13,19] -> [9,19]
  This is the top-left corner, going up to row 9.
- Points 0-1: [9,19] -> [9,48]
  This is the top edge.
So the top of the left chamber is at row 9 (not row 13 or 14). The square at row 19 is not at the top of the chamber.
Wait, but the square couldn't move UP from row 19. Let me check: the boundary has [19,9] to [19,13], which is at row 19. But the top of the chamber is at row 9. So there must be a wall or divider at row 19 that blocks upward movement.
Looking at points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13], this is a horizontal segment at row 19. And points 33-34: [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical segment at col 18-19. So there's a wall at row 19, cols 9-13, and a wall at col 18-19, rows 18-38.
I think the left chamber has a "L" shape or something similar. The upper part is at cols 19-28, rows 9-19. The lower part is at cols 9-18, rows 19-58. The square can move within the chamber, but the shape constrains its movement.
Actually wait, let me reconsider. The boundary traces the OUTER edge of the black container. The interior (yellow/orange) is INSIDE the boundary. So the boundary points define the edge between the black container and the interior.
If the boundary has [19,9] to [19,13], this means at row 19, cols 9-13, there's a transition from black to interior. So the interior is below row 19 (or above, depending on the direction).
Let me think about this differently. The boundary is a clockwise traversal. Starting from [9,19] (top-left), going right to [9,48] (top-right), then down, then left, then up, etc.
Points 49-54: [19,9] -> [19,13] -> [18,14] -> [14,14] -> [14,18] -> [13,19]
This is going from bottom-left to top-left of the left chamber. The direction is: right (19,9 to 19,13), then up-right (19,13 to 18,14), then up (18,14 to 14,14), then right (14,14 to 14,18), then up-right (14,18 to 13,19).
So the interior is to the RIGHT of this boundary (since we're tracing clockwise, the interior is on the right). This means the interior of the left chamber is at cols > 9 (for row 19), cols > 13 (for row 19), etc.
Hmm, this is getting too complicated. Let me just try practical moves and see what happens.
Key insight from the boundary: the left chamber has a narrow upper section (around cols 19-28) and a wider lower section (around cols 9-28). The square at row 19, col 19 is in the upper section.
If I move the square LEFT to col 14 or 9, it might enter the lower section, where it can move down. Let me try this!
Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- LEFT: left moves to col 14. If the chamber allows, the square might be able to move down at col 14.
- Then DOWN: if left is now in the lower section, it might be able to move down.
But wait, DOWN moves the LOWER square (higher row number). The right square is at row 54, which is lower than the left square at row 19. So DOWN would move the right square down, not the left square.
Unless... moving the left square to col 14 changes its vertical position? No, LEFT only changes the column, not the row.
Hmm, but what if the chamber at col 14 has a different vertical range? If the left square at col 14 can be at rows 19-58, then it's still at row 19, which is higher than the right square at row 54.
I think the key insight is: to move the left square down, I need it to be the LOWER square. But the right square is always at row >= 39, and the left square is at row 19. So the left square is always higher.
Unless I can move the right square ABOVE the left square. The right square's minimum row is 39 (at col 44) or 44 (at col 34). The left square is at row 19. So the right square can never be above the left square.
Wait, but what if I move the left square DOWN first? But I can't move it down because it's the higher square.
This is a true deadlock. The only way out is if there's a mechanic I'm missing.
Let me reconsider the movement semantics. Maybe UP and DOWN don't work the way I think. Let me re-examine the evidence:
From the history:
- Both at row 44: UP -> both at row 39 (both moved up)
- Both at row 39: UP -> both at row 34 (both moved up)
- Both at row 34: UP -> both at row 29 (both moved up)
- Left at 29, right at 39: UP -> left at 24, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 24, right at 39: UP -> left at 19, right at 39 (only left moved)
- Left at 19, right at 39: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 44 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 44: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 49 (only right moved)
- Left at 19, right at 49: DOWN -> left at 19, right at 54 (only right moved)
So the pattern is:
- When both at same row: both move
- When at different rows: only the one that would increase separation moves
UP increases vertical separation by moving the higher square up.
DOWN increases vertical separation by moving the lower square down.
This is consistent with my understanding. The problem is that from the current position (left at 19, right at 54), UP would move left up (blocked) and DOWN would move right down (blocked).
Wait, but what about LEFT and RIGHT? They change the horizontal positions. Maybe at different horizontal positions, the vertical bounds are different.
Let me check: at col 9, can the left square go below row 19? The boundary shows [19,9] to [19,13], which is at row 19. But the chamber continues below row 19 at cols 9-18 (based on the boundary points 41-48).
So if the left square is at col 9, it might be able to move down to rows 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, 49, 54, 58. But the issue is that DOWN only moves the lower square, and the right square is at row 54.
Unless... I move the right square to a column where its vertical range is different. For example, if the right square at col 49 has a vertical range of 29-49, then it could be at row 29, which is higher than the left square at row 19. Then DOWN would move the left square down!
Let me check the boundary for the right chamber at col 49:
- Points 3-4: [14,49] to [14,53] - top-right corner
- Points 4-5: [14,53] to [53,53] - right edge
- Points 18-19: [38,49] to [29,49] - left edge of upper-right section
So at col 49, the chamber goes from row 14 to row 53 (approximately). The right square at col 49 could be at rows 19, 24, 29, ..., 49, 54.
Wait, but the right square's minimum row is 39 (at col 44). At col 49, the minimum row might be 29 (based on the boundary point [29,49]).
If the right square can be at row 29 at col 49, and the left square is at row 19, then the right square is lower (29 > 19), and DOWN would move the right square down. But I want to move the left square down, so I need the left square to be lower.
Hmm, this still doesn't help. The left square is at row 19, which is the minimum for the left chamber. It can't go lower than row 19... or can it?
Wait, let me re-examine. The left square started at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So row 19 is the TOP of the left chamber (for the column where the square is). But at different columns, the top might be different.
Looking at the boundary:
- Points 49-50: [19,9] to [19,13] - at row 19, cols 9-13
- Points 50-51: [19,13] to [18,14] - step up
- Points 51-52: [18,14] to [14,14] - up to row 14
- Points 52-53: [14,14] to [14,18] - right to col 18
- Points 53-54: [14,18] to [13,19] - step up to row 13
So at col 9-13, the top is at row 19. At col 14-18, the top is at row 14. At col 19, the top is at row 13 (or 9?).
Wait, points 54-0: [13,19] to [9,19], this goes up to row 9 at col 19. So at col 19, the top is at row 9.
But the left square is at row 19, col 19, and it couldn't move UP. This suggests that the square is blocked from moving up, even though the chamber extends to row 9.
Oh! Maybe the square is blocked by the chamber shape. At col 19, the chamber might have a narrow passage that the square can't fit through. Or maybe there's a wall at row 19 that blocks upward movement.
Actually, looking at the boundary points 33-34: [18,19] to [38,18], this is a vertical wall at col 18-19, going from row 18 to row 38. So at col 19, there's a wall from row 18 to row 38. This would block the square from moving up from row 19.
Wait, but the square is at rows 19-23 (5x5 square). If there's a wall at row 18, the square can't move up because its top would be at row 14, which would overlap with the wall at row 18.
Hmm, but the square moved from row 44 to row 19 using UP. So it could move up to row 19. But it can't move up further because of the wall at row 18.
OK so the left square's vertical range at col 19 is 19-44 (or higher). And at col 9, the vertical range might be different.
Let me check the boundary for col 9:
- Points 42-43: [58,9] to [39,9] - left edge, rows 39-58
- Points 43-44: [39,9] to [39,13] - step right
- Points 48-49: [28,9] to [19,9] - left edge, rows 19-28
So at col 9, the chamber goes from row 19 to row 58. The square at col 9 can be at rows 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44, 49, 54.
But the square is currently at row 19, col 19. If I move it LEFT to col 14, then LEFT to col 9, it would still be at row 19. And DOWN would move the right square (at row 54) down, not the left square.
I'm stuck. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is not to move the squares to specific positions, but to achieve some other condition. Let me think about what SPACE does.
Earlier, I tried SPACE and it changed the board (board_changed: True) but didn't complete the level. Let me check what SPACE does.
Actually, looking at the current frame, SPACE might toggle something or change the HUD bars. Let me check the current state after SPACE.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the squares so they touch or overlap. Or maybe the goal is to move the squares to positions where they align with the chamber walls in a specific way.
Let me try moving the squares to their extreme positions and see if SPACE completes the level.
Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- Left is at the top of its chamber (row 19) and rightmost column (col 19).
- Right is at the bottom of its chamber (row 54) and leftmost column (col 34).
Maybe the goal is to move the squares to the opposite corners? Or to specific positions that match the HUD pattern?
Let me try: move left square to bottom-left (row 54, col 9) and right square to top-right (row 39, col 49). But I can't move the left square down...
OK, I'm going to try a completely different approach. Let me use BFS to search for a sequence of moves that gets both squares to the same row, which would allow them to move together.
State: (left_row, left_col, right_row, right_col)
Actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT
But the state space is large, and I need to know the vertical bounds at each column. Let me estimate:
- Left square: rows 19-54 (at col 9), 19-44 (at col 19)
- Right square: rows 39-54 (at col 44), 29-53 (at col 49), 44-53 (at col 34)
Actually, I realize I don't know the exact vertical bounds at each column. Let me just try to explore the state space by moving the squares to different positions and observing the results.
Plan: Move left square to col 9 (LEFT, LEFT), then try UP to see if the right square can move up (since left is at col 9, maybe its vertical range is different).
Wait, UP moves the higher square up. If left is at row 19 and right is at row 54, left is higher, and UP would move left up (blocked at row 19). So UP won't help.
Let me try: LEFT, LEFT to move left to col 9, then RIGHT to move right to col 39. Then check if the vertical bounds change.
Actually, I just realized something. The right square is at col 34, and the boundary shows the chamber at col 34 goes from row 44 to row 53. But the right square is at row 54, which is below the chamber. This suggests that the right square moved down past the chamber boundary.
Wait, maybe the boundary I'm seeing is for the current configuration, and it changes as the squares move. Or maybe the boundary includes the squares themselves.
Let me re-examine. The container boundary has 55 points. The container has 1325 pixels. The squares have 25 pixels each. So the container is much larger than the squares.
I think the boundary is the outer edge of the black container, and the squares move within the interior (yellow/orange) area. The squares can't move outside the container.
OK, let me try a practical approach. I'll move the left square to col 9 and see if its vertical range changes. Then I'll try to move it down.
But wait, I can't move the left square down because it's the higher square. The only way to move it down is if both squares are at the same row, then DOWN moves both down.
To get both to the same row, I need to either:
1. Move the left square down to match the right square's row.
2. Move the right square up to match the left square's row.
Option 1 requires the left square to be the lower square, which it's not.
Option 2 requires the right square to be the higher square, which it's not.
The only way to break the deadlock is if horizontal movement changes the vertical bounds in a way that allows one square to pass the other.
Let me think about this more carefully. If the left square at col 9 can be at rows 19-58, and the right square at col 49 can be at rows 29-53, then:
- Left at row 19, right at row 54: left is higher, right is lower. Deadlock.
- If I could move the right square to row 29 (at col 49), then right would be at row 29, which is lower than left at row 19. Still deadlock.
- If I could move the left square to row 54 (at col 9), then left would be at row 54, which is lower than right at row 29. Then DOWN would move left down, and UP would move right up.
But I can't move the left square to row 54 because it's stuck at row 19.
Hmm, what if I move the right square to a column where its vertical range includes rows below 19? For example, if the right square at col 49 can be at rows 14-53, then it could be at row 14, which is higher than left at row 19. Then UP would move the right square up (blocked at 14), and DOWN would move the left square down!
Let me check: can the right square reach row 14? The boundary shows [14,49] to [14,53], which is at row 14. So the chamber at col 49-53 has a top at row 14. If the right square can be at row 14, then it would be higher than the left square at row 19.
But the right square's current minimum row is 39 (at col 44). Can it go higher at col 49?
Let me try: move right square to col 49 (LEFT, LEFT from col 34... wait, LEFT moves right square right, so from col 34, LEFT would move it to col 39, then col 44, then col 49).
But LEFT also moves the left square left. Current: left at col 19, right at col 34.
- LEFT: left to col 14, right to col 39
- LEFT: left to col 9, right to col 44
- LEFT: left blocked at col 9, right to col 49
So after 3x LEFT, left is at col 9 and right is at col 49. Then I can try UP to see if the right square can move up.
But UP moves the higher square (left at row 19) up, which is blocked. So the right square wouldn't move.
Unless... at col 9, the left square's vertical range is different. If the left square at col 9 can be at rows 24-58 (with a higher minimum), then the right square at row 54 would be higher than the left square at row 24. Then UP would move the right square up!
But I don't know the vertical range at col 9. Let me just try it.
Plan: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT to move left to col 9 and right to col 49. Then try UP to see if the right square can move up.
Wait, but the left square is at col 19, and LEFT moves it to col 14, then col 9. After 2x LEFT, left is at col 9 and right is at col 44. After 3x LEFT, left is blocked at col 9 and right moves to col 49.
Let me try this sequence and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34)
# Plan: Move left to col 9 and right to col 49, then check if vertical bounds change

# LEFT moves: left col-5, right col+5
# From (19, 19) and (54, 34):
# LEFT 1: left (19, 14), right (54, 39)
# LEFT 2: left (19, 9), right (54, 44)
# LEFT 3: left blocked at 9, right (54, 49)

action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right: {n['boundary']}")

# Now try UP - if right square can move up at col 49, that would be progress
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 2x UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 2x UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try UP again
action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        if n['boundary'][0][1] < 30:
            print(f"Left after 3x UP: {n['boundary']}")
        else:
            print(f"Right after 3x UP: {n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Moves HIGHER square (lower row) up by 5; if tied, both move up", "DOWN": "Moves LOWER square (higher row) down by 5; if tied, both move down", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: row 19-54, col 9-19. Right: row 29-54, col 34-49. DEADLOCK at current position.", "goal_model": "Unknown - trying to find target config.", "recent_findings": ["SPACE changes board but doesn't complete level", "Need to get both squares to same row to move together"], "open_questions": ["Can right square go above row 39 at col 49?"], "current_plan": "Move right to col 49, test if it can move above row 39.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE at current config", "DOWN from deadlock", "UP from deadlock"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
